entries.ocr_text:
PROCLAMATION
TO THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE
COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY :
WHEREAS , it is apparent that the income of the State has
been very materially reduced on account of the decreased valu-
ation of all kinds of property for taxation purposes by the
State , and that a further reduction in the State's already de-
pleted revenues was occasioned by virtue of the passage by
the last Regular Session of the General Assembly of Kentucky
of laws reducing the cost of automobile and truck license
plates , and because of the reduction in the tax rate upon all
real estate within the Commonwealth from thirty cents to five
cents upon the valuation fixed thereon for state taxation pur-
poses ; and ,
WHEREAS , by virtue of the present financial condition of
the country as a whole and under the revenue laws now in
force in this State , the income of the State , for the support of
its general government and to meet the needs of government ,
has been reduced within the last two years from more than
$20,000,000 to less than $ 10,000,000 ; and , therefore , the income
of the State will be utterly inadequate to efficiently maintain
the various departments of the State Government and ade-
quately support the schools of the State commensurate with
their needs ; and ,
WHEREAS , the government of the United States , through
its Congress , has passed laws providing funds to assist the
States of the Nation in relieving the distress of their citizens ,
caused by unemployment , provided the states match the money
so provided , as required by federal authorities ; aud ,
WHEREAS , certain domestic life insurance companies ,
under present laws , are denied the privilege of borrowing
money from the Reconstruction Finance Corporation for the
reason that said companies are not authorized to issue unassess-
able stock that can be placed as collateral to secure loans from
the Federal Government ; and ,
WHEREAS , by reason of the reduction of tax upon real
estate , herein mentioned , many of the sheriffs of the State ,
elected in counties thereof, will have the emoluments of their
offices materially reduced , and to such extent that their income
will be insufficient to justify them in discharging the duties of
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, Extraordinary Session, 1934, p. 16

classification.title: Proclamation Regarding State Finances and Federal Aid
classification.summary: This proclamation from the Governor of Kentucky to the General Assembly addresses the severe reduction in state income due to decreased property valuations, lower automobile license plate costs, and a reduced real estate tax rate. It highlights the inadequacy of current revenues to support state government and schools. The proclamation also notes that the state is eligible for federal funds to alleviate unemployment distress, provided the state matches the funds. Additionally, it points out that domestic life insurance companies are unable to borrow from the Reconstruction Finance Corporation because they cannot issue unassessable stock as collateral. Finally, it mentions that reduced real estate taxes will significantly decrease the income of county sheriffs.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a proclamation from the Governor of Kentucky to the General Assembly. It discusses the state's financial difficulties, including reduced income from property taxes and license plates, and the need for federal assistance for unemployment relief. It also mentions issues faced by domestic life insurance companies and the reduced income of sheriffs. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices targeting any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
viii PROCLAMATION
such offices in the counties thus affected . These officals should ,
by virtue of the provisions of an enabling act , be relieved from
the conditions described ; and ,
WHEREAS , the regular 1934 session of the General As-
sembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky concluded its labors
and adjourned without having enacted an appropriation act,
or without having made any appropriation for the support of
the general State Governnent or any of its departments or
divisions , and so adjourned without providing any additional
revenue to meet the needs of the State Government , notwith-
standing that its depleted condition at the time was well known ,
and failed to enact any revenue measures whatsoever in lieu of
the revenues lost to the State by virtue of certain laws passed
by that session ; and ,
'WHEREAS , certain laws , that were enacted at the ses-
sion referred to , were defective on account of irregularities oc-
curring in the enrolled bill that would have destroyed their
effectiveness . These laws should be re -enacted , repealed or
amended at the ensuing special session :
THEREFORE , in consideration of the facts and conditions
mentioned , and in accordance with the power vested in me by
by the Constitution of the State to convene the General Assem-
bly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky in extraordinary session ,
I, as Governor of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , do issue
this , my proclamation , convening the General assembly of
Kentucky in extraordinary session at the seat of government
at Frankfort , Kentucky , at twelve o'clock noon , on Wednesday ,
the ninth day of May , 1934 , for the sole purpose of considering
the following subjects :
1. To enact such measures as will provide sufficient reve-
nue to carry on all proper functions of the State Government ,
including common schools and higher institutions of learning ,
and to provide necessary funds for relief of the unemployed and
distressed of the State ; and to repeal any laws in conflict with
such measures .
2. To appropriate necessary sums to the various State de-
partments , commissions and agencies to carry on their respec-
tive duties and functions .
3. To amend and re -enact or correct Senate Bili No. 330 ,
1934 General Assembly .
4. To amend and re -enact a bill introduced at the 1934
session of the General Assembly, being House Bill No. 360 , re-
lating to the consolidation of the offices of jailers and sheriffs .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, Extraordinary Session, 1934, p. 17

classification.title: Proclamation Convening General Assembly in Extraordinary Session
classification.summary: This document is a proclamation issued by the Governor of Kentucky in 1934, convening the General Assembly in an extraordinary session. The purpose of the session is to address critical financial issues, including the lack of an appropriation act, insufficient revenue, and defective laws passed during the regular session. PROCLAMATION ix
5. To enact a measure authorizing domestic life insurance
companies to issue non-assessable stock .
6. To amend , re -enact or repeal House Bill No. 781 , known
as the Nudist Bill .
7. To enact a measure compensating the sheriffs of the
State in sums not exceeding the amount lost to them in com-
missions at four per cent by reason of the reduction of the real
estate tax from thirty cents to five cents for State purposes .
8. To enact laws relating to chattel mortgages .
9. To enact a measure relating to the acceptance by the
State of the William Lindsay property , devised to it by Eleanor
Holmes Lindsay .
10. To amend and re -enact House Bill No. 477 , relating to
a refund of auto truck license fees .
You will, therefore , in accordance with this proclamation ,
assemble in said extraordinary session at the seat of govern-
ment at Frankfort , Kentucky , on the day and date above writ-
ten, to consider the subjects herein mentioned , and only said
subjects .
Given under my hand , this the seventh day of May, 1934 ,
and in the one hundred and forty -second year of the Common-
wealth .
BY THE GOVERNOR :
( Signed ) SARA W. MAHAN
Secretary of State
(Signed ) RUBY LAFFOON
Governor of Kentucky

classification.title: An Act relating to mortgages of personal property by sharecroppers and tenants
classification.summary: This act clarifies the conditions under which sharecroppers and tenants can validly mortgage personal property. It states that a landlord's release, waiver, or subordination of their right, title, claim, or interest in the property is sufficient for the tenant to mortgage it. It also allows for mortgages to secure future advances made within one year, up to a stated amount, and these advances are secured equally with the original amount. Such mortgages are valid against various parties, including creditors and subsequent purchasers.

The language is purely transactional and legal, focusing on property rights and financial agreements. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act relating to chattel mortgages
classification.summary: This act establishes the priority of chattel mortgages filed for record over unrecorded conditional sales contracts, mortgages, liens, or encumbrances. It also specifies that such mortgages can include replacements of mortgaged property, the increase of livestock, and the produce of mortgaged property. Furthermore, it clarifies that a chattel mortgage on livestock and feedstuffs is not invalidated by provisions allowing the mortgagor to use or consume the feed for the livestock's upkeep or preparation for market. It also addresses mortgages to the Farm Credit Administration and production credit associations.

It allows mortgagors to sell or exchange mortgaged chattels under specific conditions, provided the proceeds are applied to the mortgage debt or used for expenses related to cultivating, harvesting, preparing for market, processing, marketing, or preserving the remaining property. classification.summary: This act details the procedures for filing and releasing chattel mortgages with the County Court Clerk. It covers the delivery of satisfaction instruments, the filing and preservation of assignments and transfers, the admissibility of certified copies as evidence, and the issuance of receipts upon filing. It also outlines the process for filing releases, waivers, subordinations, and other instruments affecting rights to mortgaged property.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes administrative procedures for filing and managing chattel mortgages. There is no mention of race, and the subject matter (financial transactions and property law) does not inherently suggest a racial component. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute outlines provisions for chattel mortgages and deeds of trust, specifically addressing the release of liens on mortgaged property. It details how mortgagees or holders of secured debt can consent to the extinguishment of liens, release parts of the security upon payment, and how these actions are binding on purchasers and third parties. It also specifies methods for foreclosing chattel mortgages executed to secure loans from certain federal entities like the Farm Credit Administration, Production Credit Associations, and the Reconstruction Finance Corporation.

The term " borrower " where used in this Act

The term " lender " where used in this Act shall
include the original lender, its successors , trans-

and/or holder of the secured indebtedness .
The term " mortgagee " where used in this Act
shall include the original mortgagee and the owner

ministrators , executors , representatives , heirs , dis-
tributees , devisees , legatees , transferees , indorsees

The term " mortgagor " where used in this Act

CHAPTER 2 17
$ 19. Because of the fact that a serious economic
depression has adversely affected thousands of citi-
zens of Kentucky , and because of the fact that great
numbers of farmers in this state are in distressed
financial conditions , and because of the fact that it
is urgent that a great many of these farmers borrow
money immediately , and because of the fact that cer-
tain agencies of the Federal Government and units
or divisions of the Federal Farm Credit Administra-
tion are now ready , able , willing and anxious to be
of assistance to those farmers by making loans to
said farmers and take as security therefor chattel
mortgages on the subjects mentioned in this Act ,
and because of the fact that the present chattel mort-
gage law in the state of Kentucky does not offer
ample security to the said agencies of the Federal
Farm Credit Administration and prospective lend-
ers to induce them to loan moneys on such security ,
an emergency is hereby declared to exist and this
Act shall be effective immediately upon its passage
and approval by the Governor .
Approved June 25 , 1934 .

classification.title: Appropriation for Kentucky Progress Commission
classification.summary: This statute appropriates funds from the general expenditure fund to the Department of Public Property for the Kentucky Progress Commission. The funds are designated for the fiscal years ending June 30, 1935, and June 30, 1936. The purpose of the commission is to advertise Kentucky's natural resources, scenic wonders, and historic points of interest, as outlined in Chapter 163 of the Acts of 1928. The statute also specifies that no more than ten percent of the appropriated amount can be used for the salary and expenses of an executive secretary and operational overhead, and only if the Kentucky Progress Association's income is insufficient to cover these costs. Additionally, all miscellaneous receipts collected by the commission are to be appropriated and credited to the commission after being covered into the State treasury.

CHAPTER 2 101
Sec . 12 , of Article 3, Chapter 25 , of the Acts of 1934 , provides
that in the event that the General Assembly shall fail to make
an appropriation for any fiscal year for any purpose , or purposes ,
required to be executed by provisions of existing laws , or the
Governor shall veto an appropriation essential to the carrying
out of any such purpose , or purposes , then , and in that event , an
appropriation equal in amount to the appropriation made or
deemed to have been made under the provisions of this section
for such purpose , or purposes , for the fiscal year next preceding ,
exclusive of any such appropriation for extraordinary expenses
and capital outlays , shall be deemed to have been made and
shall continue available from year to year until an appropria-
tion shall have been made as provided in this act and shall be-
come effective .
Under the provisions of this Section the Department of Health
is hereby deemed to have been made an appropriation equal in
amount to operating receipts for each and every bureau and
agency provided for under sub -section L- 1 and u-1, inclusive , and
sub -sections L-2 to u-2, inclusive , of Sec . 18-A, of House Bill No.
67 , in Senate .
In vetoing sub -sections L -1 to u -1, inclusive , and sub -sections
L-2 to u-2, inclusive , Sec . 18 -A , of this Act , it is my purpose to
permit the said bureaus and agencies of the Department of
Health to operate under the provisions of Sec . 12 and 14 , of
Article 3 , Chapter 25 , Acts of 1934. (Regular Session ) .
Sec . 14 , of Article 3 , Chapter 25 , Acts of 1934 , ( Regular Ses-
sion ) provides for the establishment , by appropriation , of re-
volving fund accounts in the State Treasury to be composed of
operating receipts of industries , farms, hospitals , sanitoria ,
dining halls , dormatories , book stores , refreshment booths , ath-
letics or activities , self-supporting in whole or in part and /or
by depositing the operating receipts of such activities and agen-
cies in the State Treasury to the credit of such revolving ac-
count . The activities and services listed in this section appear
to be sufficiently comprehensive to include the services rendered
by the bureaus named under sub -section L-1 to u -1, inclusive , of
Sec . 18 -A, of House Bill No. 67, in Senate , which sub -sections I
have herein vetoed in toto .
The Department of Health may , through depositing operating
receipts from such bureaus in the State Treasury to the credit
of a revolving fund or revolving funds , withdraw the same for
the use and benefit of such bureaus .

ble , and thereby avoid duplicate expense .

without delay the sum of fifty thousand dollars is
hereby appropriated and set aside out of the Gen-
eral fund as a " Prison Revolving Fund " , which is
hereby created , and same shall be kept as a separate
fund in the state treasury and classified under the
Department of Public Welfare , and all monies col-
lected by operation of this act shall be credited to
said fund , and accumulations to the credit of said
prison revolving fund , which are not necessary
for the operation of the provisions of this act , shall
be used in defraying the expenses of the State pris-
oners and state prisons , and any excess shall be ex-
pended for the benefit of other state institutions as
the department of public welfare may determine .
The money appropriated in this bill shall be ex-
pended by the department of public welfare in its
discretion for the purpose of carrying out the pro-

classification.summary: This act repeals and re-enacts Section 547 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning stockholder liability. It clarifies that stockholders are liable for the unpaid portion of their stock and not for more than that amount. An emergency is declared for immediate effect.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 567 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to the Liability of Stockholders in Certain Corporations.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 567 to clarify the liability of stockholders in banks, trust companies, guaranty companies, investment companies, and insurance companies. It specifies that stockholders are liable equally and ratably to the extent of their stock's par value, in addition to the stock's amount. Fiduciaries holding stock are not personally liable, but their estates are. It also allows life insurance companies to issue non-assessable Preferred Capital Stock, to which the double liability does not apply, citing unusual economic conditions and the need to protect policyholders. An emergency is declared for the act to become effective immediately.

$ 25. Making False Affidavit Perjury :
Any person who shall make any false affidavit , or
shall knowingly swear or affirm falsely , to any mat-
ter or thing required by the terms of this Act to be
sworn to or affirmed , shall be guilty of perjury and
upon conviction shall be punished by fine or impris-
onment as other persons committing perjury are
punishable .
$ 26. Unlawful to Permit Unlicensed Minor to
Drive Motor Vehicle :
orIt shall be unlawful for any person to cause
knowingly permit any minor under the age of
eighteen years to drive a motor vehicle upon a high-
way as an operator , unless such minor shall have
first obtained a license to so drive a motor vehicle
under the provisions of this Act .
$ 27. Unlawful to Permit Violations of Act :
No person shall authorize or knowingly permit a
motor vehicle owned by him or under his control to
be driven by any person who has no legal right to do
so or in violation of any of the provisions of this
Act.
§ 28. Unlawful to Drive While License Sus-
pended or Revoked :
Any person whose operator's license has been sus-
pended or revoked , as provided in this Act , and who
shall drive any motor vehicle upon the highways of
this state while such license is suspended or revoked ,
shall be guilty of a misdemeanor , and upon convic-
tion shall be punished as provided in Section 29 of
this Act .
$ 29. Penalty for Misdemeanor :
(a) It shall be a misdemeanor for any person to
violate any of the provisions of this Act unless such
violation is by this Act or other law of this state de-
clared to be a felony .

CHAPTER 19 .
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation , repealing , amending
and re -enacting Chapter 101 of the Acts of the General As-
sembly of 1934 , defining passenger automobiles , providing for
registration fees of such motor vehicles and declaring an
emergency to exist.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Chapter 101 of the Acts of the Gen-
eral Assembly of 1934 , regular session , be repealed ,
amended and re -enacted so that when said section is
re-enacted it shall read as follows :
Automobiles primarily designed for carrying pas-
sengers and having provisions for not more than
seven passengers , including the operator , shall be
classed as passenger automobiles and the annual
registration fees for same shall be as follows :
For each passenger car having less than 22 horse-
power , Six Dollars ( $ 6.00 ) ; for each such car hav-
ing 22 horse -power and less than 32 horse -power ,
Seven Dollars and twenty cents ( $ 7.20 ) ; for each
such car having 32 horse -power or more , Twelve
Dollars ( $ 12.00 ) , and " horse -power " within the
meaning of this act shall be calculated on the rating
fixed by the Society of Automobile Engineers .

and Minerals should not be present ; also to assist in
such work in other districts when so directed by the
Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals ;

been sealed on account of fire or any other cause ,
when called upon by the owner or operator and when
directed by the Chief of the Department of Mines
and Minerals so to do .

of the Department of Mines and Minerals a report

examination of the interior of each mine with re-
spect to ventilation , drainage , timbering , blast-
ing , electricity and general security as well as the

§ 19. Ventilation of Mines .- Should the district
inspector discover any room , entry, airway or other
working places being driven in advance of the air
currents contrary to the requirements of this Act , he
shall notify the mine foreman to cease work in such
places at once until the law is complied with .
The ventilation of all coal mines at which as many
as five men are employed shall be produced by means
of fans mechanically operated , located outside the
mine . Mines at which less than five men are em-
ployed shall be ventilated by means approved by the
Department of Mines and Minerals . Provided , how-
ever , that when inside fans are required to ventilate
working places on longwall or other modern systems
of mining , the Chief of the Department of Mines and
Mineral must first approve such installation .
In case of accident to the ventilating fan , or its
machinery whereby the ventilation of the mine
would be seriously interrupted , the mine foreman
shall order the men to withdraw immediately from
the mine , and he shall not allow them to return to
their work until the ventilation has been restored

$ 20. Accumulation of Coal Dust .-In all mines
accumulations of fine dry coal dust shall , as far
as practicable , be removed from the mine and all dry
and dusty operating sections kept thoroughly
watered or rock dusted or dust allayed by such other
methods as may be approved by the Chief of the
Department of Mines and Minerals .
$ 21. Shafts , Slopes , and Drifts .-It shall not be
lawful for the operator , superintendent , or mine
foreman of any coal mine , whether worked by shaft ,
slope or drift , to permit persons to work therein un-
less there are to every seam of coal worked in
such mine at least two separate outlets , separated
by natural strata of not less than one hundred feet in
breadth if the mine be worked by shaft or slope , and
separated by natural strata of not less than fifty feet
in breadth if the mine be worked by drift- by which
outlets distinct means of ingress and egress are
readily available to persons employed in such mine ;
but it shall not be necessary for the two outlets to
belong to the same mine .
The foregoing requirements shall not apply to the
openings of a new entry of a mine that is being
worked for the purpose of making connection
between said two outlets so long as not more than
four persons are employed at one time in making the
connection or driving the second opening ; nor shall
said requirements apply to any mine in which the
second opening has been rendered unavailable by
reason of final robbing or removal of pillars so long
as not more than twenty persons are employed
therein at one time .
In case any coal mine has but one shaft , slope , or
drift for ingress and egress of the persons employed

§ 22. Checking Employees in and Out of Mine.—
The operator or superintendent of every mine shall
install a uniform system of checking the employees
in and out of the mine , whereby each employee upon
entering the mine shall be given a check by which
he shall be identified , and which check shall be
placed in its proper place on the check board by the
employee when leaving the mine .

stantially fixed and well protected .

$ 35. Electric Coal Cutting Machines .-In gase-
ous mines the Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals may designate where flameproof electric
coal -cutting machines shall be used .
No man shall be placed in charge of a coal -cutting
machine in any gaseous mine or gaseous portion of
a mine who is not a competent person , capable of
determining the safety of the roof and the sides of
the working places and detecting the presence of ex-
plosive gas . Machine runners shall be compelled to
undergo an examination to determine their fitness
to detect explosive gas before they are permitted to
have charge of machines in mines liberating gas ,
unless they be accompanied by a certified fire boss ,
or a machine runner or helper having passed such
an examination . Said examination to be given by
the mine foreman and approved by the District In-
spector , blank forms for same to be furnished by the
Department of Mines and Minerals , a copy to be re-
tained on file at the mine office and the original to
be sent to the State Department of Mines and Min-
erals fully made out and signed by the machine run-
ner and the mine foreman and approved by the
District Inspector .
In any gaseous portion of a mine , a coal -cutting
machine shall not be brought within the last break
through next the working face , until the machine
man shall have made an inspection for gas in the
place where the machine is to work , unless such ex-
amination is then made by some other competent
person authorized and appointed for that purpose
by the mine foreman . If any explosive gas is found
in the place the machine shall not be taken in until
the gas is removed .

owner or owners or operator or operators shall per-
mit such person to be employed by said miners mak-
ing the request . Provided , the person so employed

discreet , sober and upright man . Said check weigh-
man shall be duly elected by a majority of the em-
ployees engaged in mining and loading coal and said
election shall be properly conducted by secret ballot

Department as provided in this Act , which fee shall
be paid by said Department into the State Treasury
and credited to the General Expenditure Fund .
Whereas , the present mine scale inspection will
cease on July first and numerous other activities of
the Department of Mines and Minerals will be dis-
continued on said date , and the lives of numerous
workmen will be endangered , an emergency is de-
clared to exist and this Act will become effective
upon its passage and approval by the Governor .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

flour , label and the size of packages containing same ; and
to provide penalties for the violation of this Act .

shall be packages containing net avoirdupois
weights of two ( 2 ) , six ( 6 ) , twelve ( 12 ) , twenty -four
( 24 ) , forty -eight ( 48 ) , ninety -six ( 96 ) and one hun-
dred and ninety-six ( 196 ) pounds .

CHAPTER 23 213
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Nudists , as defined in this Act , means any
person or group of persons who display any part of
their private person naked before persons of the
opposite sex , not their husband or wife , at their
solicitation or with their consent for religious or
health purposes.
§ 2. Hereafter , any person , firm , corporation or
organization desiring to own or operate a nudist
colony must first secure a license therefor from the
State Tax Commission , and pay an annual tax there-
on of One Thousand ($1,000.00 ) Dollars .
§ 3. In order that the privacy of the person or
persons living in a nudist colony might be assured ,
it shall be unlawful for any person , firm , corporation
or other organization to operate said colony without
first building a wall twenty feet in height , made of
brick , stone or cement around the premises on which
the colony is located .
4. It shall be the duty of the owner or opera-
tor or person in charge of such colony to keep a
register in which shall be kept the names and ad-
dresses of all persons who may enter said colony ,
which register shall be open to inspection at all times
to the proper authorities .
§ 5. Said colony shall be inspected by such per-
sons and at such times as may be deemed necessary
by the Attorney General .
§ 6. Any person , firm , corporation or organiza-
tion found guilty of violating the provisions of this
Act shall be deemed guilty of a misdemeanor , and
upon conviction shall be fined not less than One
Thousand ($1,000.00 ) Dollars .
7. All laws or parts of laws in conflict here-
with are hereby repealed .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

214 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 24.
AN ACT providing for the consolidation of the office of jailer
with that of sheriff in each county of the State .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
$ 1. The office of jailer is hereby consolidated
with that of sheriff in each county of the State ,
under the provisions of Section 105 of the Constitu-
tion . There are hereby transferred to and vested
in the sheriff , all the powers and duties heretofore
authorized by law to be exercised or performed by
the jailer . Wherever in any law of the State , refer-
ence is made to the jailer , such reference shall be
deemed to apply to the sheriff , except where the
context requires otherwise .
§ 2. All laws and parts of laws in conflict with
this act , are hereby repealed to the extent of such
conflict .
§ 3. This act shall take effect at the expiration
of the term of the jailers in office when the act is
passed . Neither approved nor disapproved .
CHAPTER 25 .
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation , imposing an excise
tax on the gross receipts of certain persons , firms , corpora-
tions , associations , co-partnerships and other forms of busi-
ness organizations engaged in selling at retail certain types
of personal property and /or certain classes of services and /or
entertainment ; providing for the enforcement of said act , the
collection and distribution of the revenue arising therefrom
and repealing all conflicting laws , and declaring an emergency
to exist .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. This act shall be known and may be cited
as the Gross Receipts Tax Law of 1934.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. The word " merchant , " as used in this Act ,
shall mean and include every person , association , co-
partnership or corporation engaged in the buying
and selling of goods , wares or merchandise at any
store , stand or place in this State occupied for that
purpose . In determining the number of stores ,
stands or places of business owned or operated by
a merchant for the purpose of fixing the tax due , the
merchant having a controlling interest , directly or
indirectly , in any store , stand or place of business
owned or operated by a merchant having a con-
trolling interest , directly or indirectly , in any store ,
stand or place of business shall be liable for and
pay the tax due . Should two or more merchants , as
defined herein , own , operate or have a controlling
interest in different stores , stands or places of busi-
ness and pool their buying or selling, each and every
store , stand or place of business , so operated , shall
be treated as being owned by one merchant for the
purpose of determining the tax due.
Provided , however , the words " store , or stands
or places of business " shall not include " filling or
service stations " maintained for the servicing of
motor vehicles if at least seventy ( 70 % ) per cent of
the business done by any person , association , co-
partnership or corporation at his or its such filling
or service station and/or stations in this State are
sales of motor fuels and/or motor lubricants .
§ 2. All taxes collected under the provisions of
this Act shall be covered through the Auditor of
Public Accounts into the State Treasury , and de-
posited in the general expenditure fund .
$ 3. Every merchant owning , operating or con-
trolling any store or stores , stands or places of busi-
ness in this State , shall, on or before July 1 , 1934 ,

236 IACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 30 .
RESOLUTION providing a contingent fund for the Clerk of the
Senate and Clerk of the House .
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That for the purpose of paying such expenses as
the Chief Clerk of the Senate of Kentucky is direct-
ed to incur by the Senate of Kentucky , there
is hereby appropriated out of the General revenues
of the State , as a contingent fund , the sum of Two
Hundred Fifty ( $ 250.00 ) Dollars , and for the pur-
pose of paying the expenses incurred by the Chief
Clerk of the House of Representatives of the Com-
monwealth , under the direction of said House there
is hereby appropriated out of the General Revenues
of the State , as a contingent fund , the sum of Two
Hundred Fifty ( $ 250.00 ) Dollars . Said respective
funds shall be paid out on warrant of the Auditor ,
issued on requisition of the Chief Clerk of the Sen-
ate and of the House of Representatives , and ,
Whereas , it is necessary that the said Clerks
shall have immediate use of said contingent funds ,
Therefore , an emergency is hereby declared
to exist , and this resolution shall take effect from
and after its passage and approval by the Governor .
Approved June 29 , 1934 .

WHEREAS , all of said persons named herein , except the first
six , served as bystanders in the trial of various felony cases
where the regular panel were disqualified ; and
WHEREAS , each and all of said persons were summoned by the
Sheriff of said county under the direction of the presiding

mons and order of the Floyd Circuit Court and rendered to the
Commonwealth of Kentucky and said court faithful and effi-
cient jury service , paying their expenses while attending said
court as jurors ; now , therefore
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the

jurors of the Floyd Circuit Court for the number
of days and nights set opposite their names be paid

entries.ocr_text:
244 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
§ 1. That the State Librarian is hereby author-
ized and directed to purchase at not exceeding two
dollars per copy five hundred and fifty copies of the
book entitled " Kentucky Directory 1934 " for the
use of the Courts , state and county officers and mem-
bers of the General Assembly . The Librarian will
furnish the members of the General Assembly and
such officers as may be designated by the Presiding
officers of the respective Houses a copy of same
and will furnish the state officials with copies thereof.
and will send a copy thereof to the Clerk of the
County Court of each county in the state to be kept
in his office . The Librarian will furnish to the Li-
brarian of each state and territory of the union a
copy of said book in exchange for similar books from
said states and territories , and a copy to the Libra-
rian of Congress , and deliver to the Secretary of
State fifty copies to exchange .
§ 2. It being necessary that the officers should
have the books herein provided for , an emergency is
declared and this resolution shall take effect upon
its adoption and approval by the Governor .
Neither approved nor disapproved .
CHAPTER 33.
A JOINT AND CONCURRENT RESOLUTION providing for the
payment of money to G. 'W. Lawrence , Mrs. Ollie Brightwell ,
Fletcher Davis , Mrs. Alice Allnutt , A. J. Bauer, Dan Mahoney ,
Joe Rosson , Robert Baker , Dennis Rath , Robert Pulliam and
Forrest Sanford for extra services in caring for the offices in
the State Capitol building during the 1934 session of the
Legislature .
WHEREAS , the aforesaid persons , employees of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky , have been required to perform overtime
and extra services because of the regular 1934 session of the
General Assembly ,

entries.ocr_text:
CHAPTER 34 245
Now therefore , be it resolved by the House of
Representatives of the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky , the Senate of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky concurring therein :
That G. W. Lawrence , A. J. Bauer , Dan Mahoney ,
Joe Rosson , Robert Baker , Dennis Rath , Robert
Pulliam , Forrest Sanford , Mrs. Ollie Brightwell ,
Fletcher Davis and Mrs. Alice Allnut are each here-
by allowed the sum of one dollar ($1.00 ) per day
for each day of the regular 1934 session of the Gen-
eral Assembly , which allowance shall be in addition
to the salaries they now receive for their services .
The appropriation hereby made is to be paid out
of any funds in the Treasury not otherwise ap-
propriated , and the Auditor of Public Accounts is
hereby ordered and directed to draw his warrant
upon the Treasurer to each of the above named
persons for the amount due them as herein provided .
Approved July 2 , 1934 .
CHAPTER 34.

CHAPTER 35.
CONCURRENT RESOLUTION , declining any interest of the

WHEREAS , on the seventh day of February one thousand nine
hundred thirty -four , Eleanor Holmes Lindsay died testate , a
resident of Franklin County , Kentucky , and ,
WHEREAS , on the fifth day of March one thousand nine hun-
dred thirty -four her will was duly probated by the Franklin
County Court and recorded in the office of the Clerk of the
Franklin County Court in Will Book number seven , page four
hundred and ten , and
WHEREAS , by said will, after certain personal bequests were
made , the said testator , Eleanor Holmes Lindsay , bequeathed
all of her property , consisting of her home located on Wap-
ping and Wilkerson streets in Frankfort , Kentucky , and the
contents therein , and all personal property connected there-
with , to the State of Kentucky , and
WHEREAS , said property was encumbeerd by a mortgage unpaid
at the time of the death of the said Eleanor Holmes Lindsay ,
and
WHEREAS , at the regular session of the Kentucky Legislature
for the year one thousand nine hundred thirty -four , sitting at
that time , a bill was introduced providing that the State of
Kentucky should pay said indebtedness and take title to said
property as set out in said will, which the State of Kentucky
in said regular session declined to do , and ,
WHEREAS , a special session of the Kentucky Legislature was
called by the Honorable Ruby Laffoon , Governor of Kentucky ,
to sit at Frankfort , on May nine , one thousand nine hundred
thirty -four, and following , and among matters be called the at-
tention of the Legislature to said will and said property and
requested that they take action on said will , and
WHEREAS , a bill was introduced providing that the State of
Kentucky should take title to said property , being known as
House Bill number twenty -seven , and after due consideration
the said State of Kentucky , through its Legislature , for the
second time declined to accept said bequest and take title to
said property , so now
Therefore , be it resolved by the House of Repre-
sentatives of the State of Kentucky , the Senate con-
curring therein :
